sampleAnswer: I think it has become easier for people to maintain good health nowadays. As Claire says, economic growth enables more people to afford healthier diets and medical care. In addition, thanks to more disposable incomes, people can even spend some money on fitness facilities and professional training sessions. Though Paul does have a point when it comes to the downsides of modern technologies, the bright sides of technological progress can counteract, even outweigh, the downsides. In fact, with easier Internet access, people can get instant access to tons of health information and knowledge. Besides, gadgets like the Apple watch allow people to keep better track of their health conditions. Moreover, a lot of applications allow people to work out anywhere and anytime. In short, people today can better maintain good health than in the past.

I think economic development is still of greater significance compared to environmental protection. As Claire says, the top priority is the survival and development of humans. If human survival is at stake, there is no way that the environment can be effectively protected. Moreover, if economic growth slows down, people's living standards are going down as well, leading to fewer budgets for the construction of nature reserves or national parks intended for environmental protection and wildlife preservation. Environmental protection needs tons of money, and that comes from a prosperous economy. It is true that human survival and flourishment ride on the environment, but putting more emphasis on the economy does not mean sacrificing the environment completely. In certain phases in human history, businesses and industries developed at the expense of the environment, but it was just temporary. When humans become better off, the priority would naturally switch to balancing the economy and the environment. But still, in human society, humans’ well-being always comes first. In short, we should not slow down the economy to protect the environment.

For individuals to do in their daily lives to protect the environment, I think walking or cycling instead of driving works better than promoting organic foods. Steve did have a point when he talked about how organically grown crops help reduce water and soil pollution. However, he did not see the practicality of the approach. First, organic foods are so much more costly that they are not universally affordable for most people. Besides, organic crop yields are rather small compared to the demand. If organic foods replaced all regular foods, food supplies would be insufficient and people would starve. Lila, by contrast, proposed a much more viable option. In addition to the considerable outcome of the collective effort she mentioned, it is quite feasible. It is true that some people live far away from where they work or go to school, but not everybody does. In fact, quite a number of people live within walking or cycling distance from their workplace or school. Considering the health benefits and the universal lack of exercise nowadays, a lot of people would be fully motivated to do so. In short, walking or cycling instead of driving is a better option than promoting organic foods.

From my perspective, strict policies and laws are not the only way to enhance the recycling of household waste. Just like Mia said, no one likes legal risks and potential financial loss. However, fear-based mechanisms may fail when people figure out means to bypass laws and regulations, considering that sorting and recycling is quite a complicated chore to do. At the end of the day, laws are enforced by people, and their awareness plays an important role in the whole recycling idea. In this sense, education, as Joshua brought up, is definitely the key. I also want to add that education does not only tell people that recycling is morally right but also teaches people how to do it, such as classifying trash and what waste is recyclable, which is probably the most crucial link in the process. In fact, many people do not sort and recycle just because they lack the knowledge involved. With sufficient education gained through campaigns and public service announcements, these people will be able and thus willing to join the cause. In this case, strict regulation will not even be necessary.

I would say it is better to relax alone. Maria made an excellent point that the very act of hanging out with others can already bring stress because people have to think of others’ will over their own. I want to add that, in fact, social activities can be stressful in other another significant way, peer pressure. To be specific, people are constantly influenced by their peers, such as friends, relatives, or even strangers they see online. For example, when you see that your friend has bought something fancy, you would probably feel the need to buy similar products, and when most of your friends are married, you may feel that you should get married, too. In this sense, people receive pressure just by hanging around their peers. I do agree with Carl that some distraction is a good idea to reduce stress. However, it does not have to be achieved through social interactions. People can always shift their focus to their hobbies. In short, it is better to relax alone.

I personally would vote for Amber’s opinion. It is true that there are still numerous obstacles to maintaining good health in today's society. For example, factors such as pollution, stress, and the prevalence of unhealthy food options can lead to a wide range of health issues, from obesity to heart diseases. Also, access to quality healthcare remains an enormous challenge for many individuals, particularly those in low-income communities. Despite these challenges, it is important to acknowledge the fact that there have actually been significant advancements in healthcare and medical technology in recent years. Thanks to these advances, an increasing number of people now have access to a broader range of treatments and preventative measures, such as vaccines and screenings. Furthermore, there is a greater emphasis on healthy living, with more and more people taking an active interest in nutrition and exercise. Also, due to the rise of the internet, such information is now at people’s fingertips. 156 words

The ecological tax on air travel is necessary to address environmental problems associated with air travel.The ecological tax is a policy tool that aims to address environmental problems associated with air travel by allocating funds to facilitate less polluting modes of transportation, enhance fuel efficiency of aircraft, and develop new technologies that could mitigate the environmental impact of air travel. For instance, Sweden implemented an aviation tax in 2018 that resulted in a decrease in air travel and an increase in train travel and the use of electric cars. Additionally, certain airlines such as British Airways and Lufthansa have introduced more fuel-efficient planes to reduce their carbon footprint and offset the impact of the tax. The revenue generated from the ecological tax has been directed towards investments in climate-friendly aircraft fuel derived from renewable resources such as biomass and waste, as well as research projects focused on enhancing the efficiency of existing aircraft engines.

Admittedly, there are still challenges and barriers to overcome to achieve sustainable development goals. I agree with the positive outlook that the environmental situation is likely to improve in the near future. The increasing use of renewable energy sources, such as solar, wind, hydro, and geothermal power, is reducing dependence on fossil fuels and decreasing greenhouse gas emissions and air pollution. Additionally, sustainable agriculture practices, such as crop rotation and organic fertilizers, are preserving soil health and promoting better crop yields and biodiversity. Waste reduction and recycling are also becoming more widespread, resulting in a more efficient use of resources and less waste going to landfills. Moreover, metropolises like New York City are investing in public transportation systems and bike infrastructure to reduce private vehicle use, leading to lower air pollution and greenhouse gas emissions. The continued adoption of these sustainable practices and policies has the potential to further improve the environment and promote sustainable development. Nonetheless, it is important to continue addressing the challenges and barriers that hinder the achievement of sustainable development goals.

According to Paul, buying locally grown and organic foods can benefit the environment and human health by reducing harmful impacts. However, if we consider its implementation, consequence and feasibility, it cannot be the most useful action that individuals can take to help the environment. Organic farming practices,like crop rotation, can often result in lower yields, which can require more land to produce the same amount of food, ultimately leading to increased deforestation and even desertification in semi-arid regions. Additionally, locally grown food is not always more sustainable or appealing than imported food for environmentalists, as the environmental impact of transportation depends on several factors like distance, mode of transportation, and packaging. Furthermore, organic food tends to be more expensive than other alternatives, which may make it unaffordable for the general public, especially the disadvantaged. Therefore, buying locally grown, organic foods may not be the most sustainable or effective way to help the environment.

I agree with Paul’s stance that the government should preserve natural places such as forests. Firstly, forests are crucial natural habitats for various endangered species, many of which like ibis and panda are unique and found nowhere else on Earth. These ecosystems provide essential ecological services, including carbon sequestration, water filtration, and soil stabilization，which conserve habitable environment for endangered animls. By protecting and preserving these habitats, governments can safeguard biodiversity and ensure the continued functioning of these critical ecosystems. In addition to ecological benefits, forests also provide numerious benefits to human societies. They help regulate the Earth's climate by absorbing and storing substantial amounts of carbon dioxide from the atmosphere, and release oxygen for people to breathe. Meanwhile, forests also provide a source of organic blueberries, mushroom and ginseng，which can support local economies and allocate more funds on preserving natural forest. Additionally, the preservation of natural habitats can promote sustainable economic activities, such as ecotourism, which can generate income and employment opportunities for local communities.

While it seems reasonable that saving land for endangered animals is important because it preserves the biodiversity of the planet, it is important to note that humans are also a part of the natural ecosystem and the basic needs of the human population should be addressed. Meeting our own needs contributes to the overall health and well-being of the planet, as it creates a balanced ecosystem for human. For instance, land is necessary for housing, agriculture, and other essential purposes, which can foster a sustainable environment where people can live idyllic lives with companion animals, flowers and crops. However, we must also recognize that meeting human needs must be balanced with the conservation of endangered species and their habitats. While ignoring our needs may lead to resource scarcity, neglecting conservation efforts can lead to irreversible damage to our planet's ecosystems, with far-reaching consequences for all living organisms, including human beings. Therefore, it is crucial to find a balance between addressing human needs and conserving biodiversity.

I would subscribe to Jason’s idea that student-centered education is much preferable. In addition to the benefits of fostering a supportive community and promoting collaboration, another important point to consider relates to students’ learning preferences and the cultivation of their self-assurance during the learning journey. In a traditional lecture-style approach, the teacher asks questions at the end of the session by choosing students at random. This can be intimidating and daunting, especially if students are unsure of the answer and are expected to speak in front of the entire class. In contrast, group work and discussions provide a more conducive environment for students to build their confidence and knowledge before presenting in front of the class. By engaging in group-based conversations and activities, students would certainly feel more confident to speak to the teacher and the class. Therefore, in comparison with the lecture-style approach, student-centered education empowers students to take ownership of their learning and enhances their confidence. 159 words

I personally would vote for Amy’s opinion that history should be made mandatory for all university students. Jeremy was right when he mentioned that it may not be closely related to students pursuing certain disciplines, such as computer science and medical science. However, one should realize the fact that an overwhelming majority of college students today, including those studying science and engineering or art and music, need to do research and write a lot of papers, and attending history classes can definitely provide them with opportunities to learn how to write clearly and with good grammar, thus enhancing their academic writing abilities. Additionally, in history classes, students from different majors can learn about the opinions of historical figures or key historical events through lectures, seminars, group discussions, and academic presentations, which can enhance the persuasiveness of their arguments. Furthermore, students in different majors can enhance their understanding of their own major by studying the history of these subjects, which can benefit their future study and careers. This, without doubt, can be a great source for learning interest and motivation. 179 words

I personally would vote for Justin’s opinion that keeping primary school children in the classroom is much preferable than taking them out on field trips. Anita was right when she stressed the significance of variety in children’s learning, but the importance of their knowledge absorption, which carries more weights, should not be ignored. In fact, the knowledge acquired in the classroom is more systematic and accurate than that gained in field trips. For example, in some classes, such as mathematics, the teacher can explain a concept and demonstrate the process about using of a formula to solve a question directly on the whiteboard. Also, compared with outdoor activities such as climbing and camping, classroom learning is less exhausting for students and more conducive to their knowledge absorption. A second point relates to the safety issues involved in school field trips. There can be problems with taking large groups of students out of the class during the day as students are likely to get lost or injured, making classroom-based lessons safer than field trip alternatives. 174 words

I personally would vote for Joe’s opinion that primary schools should not promote the use of video games as educational aids. In addition to the possibility of children’s addiction to video games and display of aggressive behavior, constant exposure to games can easily lead to psychological problems, making students become antisocial. It is widely acknowledged that there is a correlation between excessive video game use and social isolation, depression, and anxiety in children. Furthermore, the negative impact that is worth mentioning is about the impairment of children’s health. If children stare at the e-screen for a long time in and after class, chances are that they gradually suffer poor eyesight and obesity, a result from decreased physical activity. Alice did make a point when emphasizing the attention-grabbing feature of video games in children’s learning. However, she fails to realize the fact that long-term playing of electronic games can actually create a wrong sense of belonging to the virtual world, causing students to lose interest in learning real-world knowledge, thus worsening their academic performance. 173 words

I am more inclined to agree with Claire that parents should make friends with their kids. As friends, parents would not be feared by their children, which is easier for both parties. Parents can show their affection for children without constraint, and children are less likely to grow into shy or even timid adults. Also, if parents can offer help and suggestion whenever needed as friends, children would not be overwhelmed by stress or slip into the realm of criminals. As for rules and discipline, if parents treat their children as equal friends, they can more effectively establish common rules for them both to follow. It is true that parents as masters can force kids to abide by the rules set for them. But children might not understand such rules, not to mention sincerely approving of them. As a result, children might just be acting good kids at home, the dominion of their parents. Once out of their parents' sight, they might do even more bad things just for fun or excitement.

I don’t think we need to have similar interests to be friends. Like my best friend Precious and I, she studies biochemistry and is not that sporty, while I’m athletic. We are completely different, but we are great friends. And I agree with Jacobs that mutual exchange of something is the most important thing in deciding friendship. But we don’t necessarily have to help each other out every time the other person is in trouble, it’s just not practical. In my opinion, this is a realistic world, whether we could facilitate each other’s development determines how long we would maintain the friendship. We need listen to each other and cooperate with each other and move toward a better future. Conversely, if we hang around with weasels and losers, we would be spiraling down and all whine about how wretched life is. But in the long run we are not going anywhere and it’s painful. So when we have friends who want the best for the best part of us, we can be together for a long time, probably through our life time.

I agree with Helen that honesty is the best policy. Lying usually jeopardizes friendship. I admit that sometimes white lies serve as social lubricant like Tim has said. But even seemingly harmless lies can have unforeseen consequences. For example, when our friends discover the deceit, they are more likely to feel hurt. Because it makes them feel that we are having a condescending attitude that he or she is not capable of handling life’s strikes or turbulences. In addition, after the initial deceit, we need to tell more lies to cover up the first one. And it finally leads to a vicious circle where we just can not tell truths from falsehoods because there are so many lies. Just think about this question: are we willing to give our friends the right or authority to lie to us whenever they think it is for our own good? I think the answer is probably negative.

I totally agree with Zoe when he said that careless parents should not be the only one to be blamed when it comes to children obesity. In fact, schools should bear the primary responsibility. I would like to add that in addition to the unbalanced diet provided by the school, which contains convenience foods, such as frozen dinners, salty snacks, and caned pastas, schoolwork burden also deprives children of the time for extracurricular activities. Such exercise burns calories and helps children maintain a healthy weight. Unfortunately, because of too much homework, children who are not allowed to be active may be less likely to burn extra calories through sports, time on the playground, or other forms of physical activity. Then it is no wonder that the sedentary lifestyle contributes to unhealthy weight gain.

I admit Eric’s point that those celebrities who become aware of their previous errors and turn back from the wrong path are admirable and continuously serve as role models for youngsters who are struggling with the same issues. However, Eric seems to have overlooked a fact that celebrities influence teens in other ways as well. When stars post images of themselves drinking or smoking on social media, they normalize substance use. Furthermore, they make it appear attractive and cool. Moreover, teens often idolize celebrities and want to be like them. This is one way that social media can have a negative impact on how young people look at themselves and the world.Therefore, if they see images on Instagram of a favorite singer or actor using drugs or drinking, they might be tempted to do so as well. Therefore, I strongly believe that celebrities can greatly mislead the behavior of young people. (151)

I think monetary motivation is the most direct and effective measure to boost birth rates. Populations are ageing in many countries thanks to better living and medical conditions while there are fewer and fewer young people, who are supposed to form the workforce. In some sense, societies are indebted to new parents for their "replenishments" of their depleting workforces. As Claire and Paul have mentioned, both men and women endure a lot both mentally and physically in raising children, so they deserve a generous reward from the society. Such rewards are also urgent to most parents, who have to pay for milk powder, diapers, childcare, etc. while having to suffer possible income reduction due to shortages of working hours and energy. Critics might argue that giving new parents money is unfair and wasteful, since raising children should be kept a domestic matter. But actually such rewards are but a small price to pay to maintain a healthy and sustainable population.

I think the lesson that matters most is that do not compromise basic stuff in life. There are some basic foundations crucial to a healthy, passionate, meaningful, and joyful life. The basics include: having fun, connecting with others, and taking regular exercise. To start, having fun is an antidote to stress. When we are having fun, we are in the moment and is present with whatever we are doing. This is pretty relaxing because we are so immersed that we temporarily forget all our stress. In addition, connection with others offers us a wonderful feeling of having a shared experience. We would feel happy and illuminated. Research has shown that socially-isolated people are likely to become more closed and addicted to negative stuff like misinformation or vicious rumors on the internet. Finally, exercise is magic. I’m so glad that my parents signed me up for a gym when I was only 13. After having worked out for like four years, I’m not only feeling better and energetic, but my memory seems to be working better. My focus is working better. So I think this philosophy of never compromising life basics is probably the most transformative thing that parents can teach their children.

I think whether we accept parents’ help depends on the extent of our maturity. When we are very young, like under the age of 15, parental decision is probably good. Just like if my parents had not forced me to learn piano at the age of 4, I would not have mastered it like I do today and gain much confidence from it. If I were allowed to make my own decision, none of the skills that I have today would probably be gone because I didn’t have the self-discipline needed when I was a child. Everything beneficial for my future that I have accomplished is under my parents’ guidance and supervision. However, the hard truth is, we would all someday outgrow our parents at certain point when our parents actually don’t know any more than we do. I know this sucks, but it’s a great opportunity for us to establish a more individual relationship with our parents. We become capable of making our own decisions and parental “advice” would probably become a hurdle in our path. Of course, to avoid hurting our parents’ feeling, we could learn to artificially maintain our independency, but just momentarily or when it’s necessary.

I disagree with Jack, when others challenge our beliefs, we should be aware that our ideas are not our identity. And what we believe is not who we are. In such case, I think we just need to be curious towards new and novel things, and think like a scientist. Basically what scientists do is they make hypotheses and they try to find evidence to either prove or disprove their own hypotheses. In this way, they are not trying to prove they are right or intellectually superior, they are just improving themselves. So I do think Ross is right, we need to be open-minded towards different ideas, they offer us the opportunity to grow and evolve. For example, from the news, we learn that some people believe that vaccination is a kind of conspiracy. They don’t get vaccinated, nor do their children. And I used to be one of them. But after the Covid, I start to rethink the situation that whether there will be any chance that I will get a COVID vaccine in my lifetime. I start to weigh the pros and the cons. If Covid has a 100% fatality rate and is highly transmissible, I probably would go for it. To sum up, changing our mind does not always make us happy, but it’s the right thing to do to be more adaptable in this world.

In my opinion, Ben is right when he says that for children who are not good at socializing, staying at home is more comfortable. By playing games in the virtual world, they get rid of the pressure from schoolwork or get a sense of achievement after completing the final stage. On the other hand, from my perspective, online games can only be a small part of children's lives. If they are considered as the only leisure activities for children, the problems are also enormous. Specifically, immoderate dependence on electronic games not only leads to health issues such as vision problems and obesity, but also affects children's personality development, leaving them at a loss when communicating face to face with others. Therefore, I think that it is necessary for parents to force their children to engage in outdoor activities, through which children develop social skills that would benefit them even when they grow older. (152)

It is true that judging the accuracy of news is quit challenging for ordinary people, as Kathy mentioned, but she might ignore the fact that modern people are capable of referring to different media outlets to seek the truth. I am of the opinion Lysol gave that it is important to learn from others' experience and prevent a repetition. The famous example is the Tsunami that happened in Japan years ago, which made many families torn apart and buildings washed out. That is when the public came to realize the great dangers embedded in nuclear power. Governments in different countries made necessary inspection in all the nuclear power plants to make the place safer. What I want to add is that the habit of reading news also improves our academic performance. If we have an intense inclination of the news happened in the global world, even though they seem have little influence on our daily lives, such news concerning economy, military and politics is likely to trigger our interests to acquire more knowledge in a broader domain. Thus, it could be noted that events happen in other countries can surely teach us a lesson and help us in academic development. (200)

Personally speaking, I believe that a deeper understanding of another culture is conducive for our personal development due to the fact that we will be more acceptable and respectful to different cultures. Obviously, the historical development of different cultures varies from many aspects, which means that knowing nothing about cultural diversity will definitely hinder our long-term development since the society is never monocultural. Accepting the cultural differences can effectively help us be more adaptive, especially considering the spread of globalization. On top of that, respecting the tradition and conventions of another culture plays a vital role in our interaction with people from distinctive cultural backgrounds. With the advancement of society, it is common that we many encounter people coming from another cultural background during our academic life or career. Thus, having a general understanding of another culture is crucial for us to achieve academic and career success. Therefore, undoubtedly, knowing about another culture will effectively contribute to our self-improvement.

Personally speaking, I believe that interacting with people from another cultural background on the online social media is the most effective and efficient method to get an in-depth comprehension of another culture. Obviously, with the widespread of internet, the online social network provides us with an appropriate platform and a comfortable environment to contact people from diverse cultural backgrounds. There is no doubt that most people lack of the opportunity to communicate with foreigners coming from a different culture in real world. However, with the aid of online social media, it is possible to connect with strangers, including those from another country. Thus, Keeping the constant interaction and establishing a stable relationship with a person from another culture is conducive for us to expand our knowledge in this culture. Accordingly, regarding the busy schedule of individuals living in modern society, connecting with local people from a different culture under the help of online social network can be considered as the most effective approach to learn about another culture.

I strongly agree with Claire’s idea that our careless use of automation has already taken a toll on our culture. In contemporary society, people have been interacting with intelligent, automated machines in nearly every aspect of their lives, whether it be shopping, banking, or the use of a telephone. The effect of this is obvious: basic respect for our fellow man is all but absent today because of increased interaction with automation. Why treat a machine with kindness? Why speak to a machine politely? It suffers no emotional or psychological damage. In a culture saturated with automation, we get used to treating machines rudely, and this may develop into a habit. In the end, we begin to treat each other in our daily life rudely. This of course leads to all sorts of issues, like intolerance and incivility, and in the long run, results in the complete degradation of culture.

I agree with Claire when she said that technology had become part of our life. Although I can see Paul’s point that people have spent much time on technology, digital devices actually provide people with more access to have a better understanding of the world. This is why we should not get rid of technology. For most people, they have little opportunity to go out of the city they live in, but with digital devices, like cellphones or iPads, they may watch videos shared by people from different areas and get to know how other people live in remoted countries. An app called Tik-tok is an example in case. Users of Tik-tok upload the video clips, which include contents of their life, work, study and so on, and other users can watch those videos and make comments to interact with the video authors. This greatly broaden people's horizons without traveling around.

I agree with Claire when she mentions some drawbacks of early exposure to technology. But technology can also be a powerful tool to help children develop certain essential skills and can be beneficial to children’s growth and education. Here I want to mention that some video games can help the development of children’s brain. For instance, in the military and shooting games, players need to get through the missions, learn all about the different weapons, beat the bad guys and eventually win the game. Players need to make plans, customize characters’ appearance and carry out a certain combat strategy. In sports games, players might come up with new tricks on the snowboard or skateboard. The creativity that one develops through playing video games can pass through to one’s abilities in life. Those who play games when they are young will be able to find a new way to handle tough situations in real life.

I agree with Claire when she mentions the convenience brought by communication technologies. But let's not ignore that communication technologies also distract us in all aspects of our life. As a matter of fact, social media have become a source of procrastination. Who hasn’t been in an office where employees often check Facebook, WeChat, or Twitter every five minutes? Even students will pause to frequently monitor their cellphones to check messages when doing homework. It is obvious that these distractions can lower people's working efficiency and quality. Worse even, in many cases, people can be so addictive to chatting with their friends or even strangers online that they totally forget their work or study. With communication technologies, hardly can people fully concentrate on their tasks, and eventually, this kind of excessive obsession with social networks can have extreme negative impacts on our lives and distract us from important things we should have focused on.

I agree with Claire’s idea that mobiles phones should be limited in public settings. Although I can see Paul’s point that some people need mobile phones to deal with important issues in their work or life, I want to stress another potential negative impact of using phones publicly: certain phone-related behaviors might be annoying. A traveler who interrupts the experience of other passengers on a crowded train by shouting into their phone, playing music or Tik-Tok videos, and playing video games loudly is considered a noise generator. In places where silence is necessary for concentration, such as a movie theatre or a library, the noise from mobile phones can be exceedingly distracting. Last week I went to the theatre. I had a very good seat, and the play was fascinating, but I did not enjoy it. A young man sitting behind me was talking loudly on his phone, so I could not hear the actors.

I think this depends on whether this country or city is economically developed. On one hand, if we are talking about a place that is fully developed, like cities in the western Europe, then there is no doubt the government should spend money on preserving old buildings. Take Norway. The Norwegian government spends millions of dollars per year on the preservation of old buildings and artifacts, since it doesn’t have to allocate too much a proportion of its financial budget to economic growth. On the other hand, if the region in question is still hovering on the brink of poverty, it would be insane to ask its government for more than spending money on feeding and sheltering its people. For example, we know that there are some African countries out there still fighting disease, famine and war. Even though they have some really old historical buildings and sites, what benefit would preserving them bring if there’s no people there to appreciate them eventually?

Learning world history is indeed quite significant for students, but I don’t think all students should be required to learn about it. It should depend on whether the student’s major or future career has anything to do with it. For one thing, if a student majors in such fields as art, literature or design, then learning world history should be taken into consideration because it would inspire them to create better works. Knowing what has happened where and when in world history would encourage these students to think and try to draw from the past experience. On the contrary, if a student majors in chemistry or physics, what really matters to them would be hands-on experience, like experimenting over and over again before coming to the final conclusion. Learning all about world history and what people before them have done wouldn’t really help if they don’t carry on by themselves. Therefore, I believe not all students should be required to learn about world history.

At present, everyone has the chance to get to know about foreign cultures though the internet or even experience them in person. I think we should keep a clear mind about foreign cultures and learn from the better ones. Some customs and traditions from other countries provide different perspectives that our indigenous ones don’t possess. In this case, we shall be completely open to welcome and learn from them. For instance, the western culture embodies more openness and inclusiveness compared with the eastern culture. Wouldn’t it be nice to try to add some new features to the local culture? However, there are still some cultures that may represent ignorance, exclusiveness and even brutality. When we are faced with such cultures, we should always stick to our own beliefs and ideas and avoid being influenced by them. In conclusion, I don’t think foreign cultures would undermine our own culture. But I do think we should be open-minded about learning from them.

Honestly, I think it depends on the value a country’s history can offer its people. For one thing, if the country in question has had quite successful experience in particular areas, then it is safe to learn from its past. For example, if the United Kingdom wants to make a difference in the ongoing Industry 4.0, it sure can draw some ideas from its own past, since the very first industrial revolution started right there in England. For another, when a country failed in or knew next to nothing about a specific aspect, perhaps it is not wise to derive anything from its own history. Instead, it might as well refer to other countries’ experience or just move on on its own. For instance, there are some countries in this world that had the awful experience of being colonized by intruders. It is best, in my opinion, for these countries to move on and learn from powerful countries so that they wouldn’t be facing the same fate ever again.

I think the internet has both positive and negative influence on appreciating and preserving cultural heritage. It is true that the internet has made it a lot easier for people to approach cultural heritage. With a lot more attention from people at every corner around the globe, watching through a screen, the awareness of saving and preserving cultural heritage has been dramatically strengthened. However, the internet also poses a great threat to cultural heritage. Because it is so easy to have access to artifacts, works and sites on the internet, many people tend to not go to the actual site or museum and would forget about the feeling of seeing the cultural heritage with their own eyes, which is especially damaging for people who want to make a difference and do something more than just seeing, like us young people. Only when we see it in person could we be so touched and shocked that we want to devote ourselves in protecting and preserving cultural heritage.

It is true that social media can offer opportunities for us to connect with old friends and strengthen our bond. However, I think that uploading records on social media also leads to security problems at the same time. By that I mean every time people post a photo or update their status, this may open the door to fraud, identity theft, and breaches of privacy. My younger sister’s experience can serve as an example. She often posted some pictures of her personal life on a social media application called Instagram, ranging from traveling experiences to birthday parties at her apartment. However, this made it possible for some frauds to collect her personal information (e.g. her family address and mobile phone number), which may pose a potential danger to her and even her relatives. Later on, as she posted more and more pictures containing her personal information on the internet, many frauds managed to steal her identity and cheat other people.

I believe that taking a break from the news can be quite beneficial. I'd add that one benefit is that taking a break from news can help people stabilize their mood and stop negative emotions. To further illustrate, the contents of news stories can vary significantly, which means that news audience are in fact on an emotional roller coaster when they are reading such news. To be specific, many detailed reports on social issues are dreadful; some political news can just be confusing and annoying; one negative comment on social media could simply put one in a bad mood. Negative moods like these can be counterproductive. If people choose to stop getting news information for a while, they can stop getting involved in an overload of information, regain a sense of inner peace and take a healthy mental break. Overall, taking a break from the news can have numerous mental health benefits.

I strongly agree with John's idea that most advertisements are misleading. I'd add that most advertisements make products seem much better than they really are, and this raises ethical concerns. Samantha's point that advertising is necessary for competition is valid, but she didn't acknowledge the negative impact it may have on consumers. She also didn't mention the importance of implementing regulations to prevent false claims that deceive customers. For instance, Advertisements create a perception of perfection, appealing to our desires and emotions. This can lead to consumers making purchases that do not match the advertised product's actual quality or functionality. This can be especially harmful when it comes to products like food and beauty products, which can impact a person's health and well-being. Overall, I believe that companies should prioritize ethical practices and transparency in their advertising campaigns, even if it means being less competitive.

In my opinion, social media has become an integral part of our lives and has significant impacts, both positive and negative ones on society, and basically, this depends on how it is used. On the positive side, it has brought people closer together, making it easy to stay in touch with friends and family who may be far away. It also provides a platform for businesses to connect with customers, and enables individuals to express themselves creatively. However, there are also negative impacts, including the spread of misinformation, cyberbullying, and addiction. Social media can also lead to feelings of loneliness and social isolation, as individuals spend more time on their devices than interacting with others face to face. Worse, social media can perpetuate the feeling of FOMO (fear of missing out). When people see their friends and acquaintances participating in activities without them, it can cause feelings of loneliness and exclusion.

In my view, artificial intelligence (AI) impacts society in both positive and negative ways, depending on how it's applied. On the positive side, AI improves efficiency and productivity across a broad range of industries, from healthcare and education to manufacturing and transportation. For example, AI-powered systems can help doctors make more accurate diagnoses or help students learn at their own pace through personalized learning software. On the other hand, there are concerns about AI displacing human workers and leading to significant job loss. Additionally, some worry about AI's potential for misuse, such as the development of autonomous weapons or the misuse of personal data. One effect of the misuse of personal data is that it can lead to serious violations of privacy and security. If personal data is collected and used without someone's consent, it can lead to a wide range of negative consequences. (144 words）

Personally speaking, I believe that the benefits brought up by playing video games outweigh the drawbacks, especially considering children’s long-term development. In addition to relaxation, playing video games can also contribute to children’s brain training and overall development. For one thing, by remembering the complicated operation rules in games, children’s hand-eye coordination can be improved and their ability to fulfill multi-tasks can also be effectively enhanced. For another, playing video games provides children with a great opportunity to interact with others and establish new relationships in the virtual world, which is helpful for them to relieve the stress gained from study and develop their social skills. By interacting with people in different age groups or from diverse cultural background, children can learn to be more acceptable and respectful in their future interpersonal relationships. Although the potential negative impacts caused by the addiction of playing video games cannot be ignored, the problem can be effectively avoided with the supervision of teachers and parents, especially when stricter regulations are set. Therefore, it is obvious that playing video games exert more positive influences on children.

As for me, rewards play a crucial role in motivating individuals to achieve greater productivity. While interest and engagement are important factors in productivity, individuals may not always have the luxury of working on tasks that align with their interests. In such cases, rewards such as bonuses, pay increases, or promotions can provide a necessary boost to motivation and lead to increased productivity. Distractions can also hinder productivity, but noise-canceling headphones can help minimize them. In fact, rewards can actually provide a valuable incentive to stay focused on tasks. For instance, a $2,000 bonus for completing an essay before the deadline would motivate an individual to concentrate on the task, regardless of any distractions present. Overall, rewards play a critical role in motivation and productivity. By providing meaningful incentives, rewards can help individuals stay focused and motivated, even when working on tasks that may not align with their interests.

I agree that asking friends for job leads is the most effective way to find employment in a new city, as Sara suggested. This approach not only gives an advantage over other candidates, but it also provides reliable information about a position. Recruitment ads can be misleading, exaggerating details to attract candidates, and online job postings can be outdated and irrelevant, as I experienced last year. I spent three days contacting recruiters and communicating about work details, only to find out that many of the job postings were no longer relevant. As for directly sending resumes to companies, it can be ineffective as well since there is no guarantee that they are hiring. Therefore, networking with friends and acquaintances offers valuable insights into the hiring process, making it the most reliable way to tap into job opportunities in a new city.

Although I agree with David that being diligent and dedicated are crucial for success in one's career, I don't think work should always take priority over everything else. Indeed, work is an important part of our lives that provides a foundation for a decent life, helps us develop skills in various fields, and can bring a sense of fulfillment. However, placing too much emphasis on success can cause stress and even lead to utilitarianism, where people only value things that are useful to them. Nowadays, sudden death has become more common in our society, and indifference between people is widespread. Besides, many families are separated, with children left behind and parents waiting for a hard-earned family reunion. In fact, people's wholehearted devotion to their jobs is not making them happier. Therefore, I agree with Emily that a positive work-life balance is key to a fulfilling life. With more peace, tranquility, and support from family and friends, people tend to have higher satisfaction with both their life and work, which can lead to better physical and mental health and even higher productivity at work.

In today's fast-paced work culture, many employees desire a shortened workweek to achieve a better work-life balance. However, if their salary is reduced accordingly, the benefits of such an arrangement may be overshadowed. While as Sarah suggests, shorter working hours could lead to higher productivity, from my perspective, this is not always the case in reality. More likely, a reduction in work hours could lead to indolence and a lack of dedication, ultimately lowering performance levels. As a result, employers may not be willing to pay the same amount of compensation for reduced output. Moreover, there are always individuals who are willing to sacrifice personal leisure time in exchange for higher pay or promotions. If a less demanding work schedule becomes widespread, these employees may be at a disadvantage in the job market. Considering these factors, while a shortened workweek may seem like an attractive option, it is important to carefully consider the potential consequences before pursuing such an arrangement.

Although Karen suggests that job-sharing is an ideal way to achieve work-life balance, as Tom points out, it is more likely to have negative impacts on individuals and the economy as a whole. While sharing a job with a partner can allow for responsibilities such as taking care of children and elderly parents, pursuing hobbies and interests, and spending more time with family and friends, these benefits may be outweighed by the decrease in job security. This is because job-sharing inevitably requires more time spent synchronizing work progress, which can lead to lower productivity and dissatisfaction from supervisors. This, in turn, may increase the risk of losing one's job when sharing responsibilities with a partner. Moreover, if job-sharing becomes prevalent across various industries, productivity throughout society may be reduced, impeding long-term economic growth. Therefore, the potential benefits of job-sharing are likely to be overshadowed by its drawbacks.

Personally speaking, I believe that it is of great significance to concern about environment when developing the economy. The primary reason is that environment preservation can guarantee the long-term prosperity of economy. Undoubtedly, economic growth relies on the natural resources, ranging from the raw materials such as metals to the fossil fuels. That is to say, the over-exploitation of the nature will eventually impose tremendous negative impacts on sustainable and long-lasting economic development. Another conspicuous reason is that ignoring the devastation of environment caused by economic advance will cause harm to citizens ‘physical health, which will impede the social development. As is known to all, the air pollution and water contamination brought up by the agricultural and industrial development is detrimental to humans’ physical well-being. Obviously, concerns about environment protection should be emphasized considering the survival of humans and the progression of society. Therefore, though it cannot be denied that economic thriving is of vital importance, environment preservation should never be neglected.

I think foresight is the most important for an entrepreneur to succeed. The business world is changing so fast, and the competition is so fierce, that whoever senses a chance and takes actions first is to succeed. That is not all, even after a startup business has grown to a giant, its boss still has to constantly detect any change that could bring about challenges and crises. Whenever a boss becomes complacent or loses his/her "touch", he/she could soon slip back to failure. My dad owns a mid-size IT company. He might not be programming all day long in front of his desk, but he has to analyze the latest trends and emerging competitors and take corresponding actions swiftly to maintain vitality and competitivity of his company. So, successful bosses might not have heavy workloads, and could seem "idle" most of the time compared to workers, but their high incomes are to a large extent rewards for their valuable foresight.

In the wake of globalization, no nation, poor or rich, can thrive without mingling into the global markets. Developing countries can make much bigger profits by exporting their cheap goods than only selling domestically. At the same time, their citizens have nearly the same opportunity to enjoy the latest industrial products as people in developed countries. In Asian countries, millions of young workers are working in assembly lines of products for export and enjoying electronic and fashion products imported from the West, which would have been impossible were it not for international trades. Another much farther-reaching benefit brought by participating in international trades is technology spillover, that is, developing countries attract multinational companies to open local branches and learn from them. For example, China has become a big manufacturer and exporter of cars by first cooperating with major car brands from developed countries and picking up relevant technology during such cooperation. To wrap it up, I do not deny the existence of sweatshops in developing countries, but it is a temporary situation and necessary price to eventually become competitive.

I agree with Andy's statement that unhealthy foods are harmful to human health, but a small amount does not affect the body much. To purchase what kind of food depends on individual choices, not what shops provide. Even if shops are no longer allowed to sell unhealthy foods, I believe that people who are addicted to alcohol and fried foods can still find ways to purchase them. What’s worse, it would leave people a feeling that their freedom of choice was taken. In addition, to curb junk food consumption would be a complete failure. This is because it can be difficult to determine where to draw the line as to what constitutes junk food. Foods vary considerably in terms of their nutritional value. Thus, it could be challenging to create a universal definition of what junk food is. Therefore, a better way to handle junk food is to make laws more strict about ingredient labels. Perhaps require foods with little nutritional value to have their nutrition facts be bolder and in a bright colour.

I totally agree with Tom’s idea. Although the increased use of cars has caused serious consequences for our environment, it is unrealistic for the government to put a ban on car ownership or use since cars have been so important in our daily life. When it comes to convenience, private transportation should be placed at the top of the list. Cars and motorcycles can make people move freely for leisure activities such as shopping, visiting friends, and traveling without depending on fixed schedules of public transportation systems or waiting for the bus in the cold or in the burning sun. Also, more people live far away from their workplaces due to the expansion of cities and towns. Driving to work is one of the most reliable and efficient choices that they have if they want to arrive at work on time.

I totally agree with Emily’s idea that the city should transform the big building into a modern shopping and entertainment center. First of all, large shopping centers can provide citizens with a rich place for leisure and entertainment. More specifically, a modern shopping and entertainment center may be a large business district integrating shopping, movie theaters, and restaurants. People no longer have to drive miles to meet different shopping needs. At the same time, this practice will have a positive impact on the local economy. On the one hand, large shopping malls can meet the needs of people in younger age groups, which can greatly increase the number of shoppers and promote local economic development. In addition, such a large business district will provide more jobs for the local government and help the government deal with the problem of high unemployment. Therefore, it is not difficult to find that transforming large buildings into modern shopping centers not only enriches the lives of citizens but also helps the healthy development of the local economy.

I totally agree with Sarah’s idea. First, the government should provide financial support for those aged over 70. Because they are old and have lost the ability to work, it is difficult for them to find a job where they can support themselves. To make matters worse, they face no income while dealing with the various diseases that come with aging organs. There is no denying that the cost of treatment for many serious diseases is very high. No money means they can only wait for death, which is very inhumane. In addition, the unemployed are only temporarily out of work. The government should provide more job opportunities for those people instead of simply providing money support. Giving money to people in the labor force will only make them overly dependent on the government and even refuse to work if they are offered jobs in the future. In the worst case, governments will bear a huge economic burden.

I totally disagree with Sarah’s idea. The government forces polluting businesses to pay excessive taxes as punishments, and these businesses will have no other choice but increase the prices of products or services to cover higher costs, and this in turn will painfully hurt poor consumers' pockets in the end. Based on what has been discussed above, I do believe that taxing Polluting Companies is not a cure-all solution. In other words, for the government to keep pollution at bay, better solutions ought to lie elsewhere. The government should help those enterprises with heavy emissions to carry out technological innovation, upgrade the equipment to filter emissions, and make the wastewater or gas discharged by enterprises cleaner. Even if new filtration equipment cannot be invented in the short term, the government can help companies improve the utilization of raw materials to achieve less pollution.

I strongly disagree with Jennifer’s idea because this type of prohibition is practical only when its foreseeable contribution definitively outweighs the drawbacks it may provoke. However, the extra tax will inevitably make certain food production companies unviable and even expose the whole society to the sadness of unemployment. Specifically, companies related to snacks, alcohol, or soda would immediately go out of business because they could not afford to pay the additional taxes. In this case, a steep swoon in food production companies is starting to put large numbers of laborers out of work but fewer skilled employees, which will suddenly increase the pressure on the government and human resources departments. However, this is not the whole story. When these economic giants fall, the country will suffer a fearful economic recession, because these catering companies are also important customers of raw material enterprises. Once losing big customers, raw material companies will face bankruptcy and job losses. As a result, countries implementing this policy will be trapped in a vicious circle and suffer economic downturns for the foreseeable future.

Personally speaking, I believe that it is not a wise move for the government to pay for all university students’ tuition fees. Admittedly, if the government distribute sufficient funds, the economic stress for students from less advantageous background can be alleviated, However, this will absolutely increase the government’s financial burden, leading to the result that less funding can be devoted into other areas such as public transportation, medical care and environment conservation. Considering the limited budget of government revenue, it is not reasonable to invest such a large amount of money in paying for college tuitions. Besides, for those students from rural and remote areas, in addition to scholarship and fellowship, they can also earn money by taking part-time jobs, which can effectively enrich their life experience and cultivate their independent ability. Therefore, there is no need for the government to allocate a large sum of money to pay for the tuition fees for all college students.

I totally agree with Paul. A most widely accepted justification of governments' existence is that they could provide certain services, including order and safety. Citizens pay for such services with tax money and, although rarely noticed by citizens, part of their rights and freedom. From our own experience, it is sadly the case that not many people have a strong awareness of safety. Standing in the streets, one can see that cyclists rarely wear helmets, so it is for the best if governments shoulder the responsibility to persuade, or force, them to wear helmets. It would be the incompetency of governments if a significant number of citizens should get injured in or die of such easy and frequent negligence of transportation safety. Also, I am afraid I have to say that transportation safety precautions are not matters that "do not involve others" as Claire put it. It is those who care about him/her or those related to him/her that really have to bear the consequences if one gets seriously injured, paralyzed, or killed in car accidents for not taking sufficient safety measures.
